dare.description: The basic question word for 'who.' Used in casual and neutral situations. For polite contexts, use どなた (donata) instead.

examples.jp: だれがきましたか
あのひとはだれですか

dare.notes: Often followed by the particle が (ga) when asking who did something.

donata.description: The polite form of 'who.' Use this when speaking to strangers, elders, or in formal situations. Much more respectful than だれ.

examples.jp: どなたですか
どなたにおあいですか
examples.en: Who would you like to meet?

donata.notes: Always use donata in customer service, business, or when speaking to someone older.

nani.description: The question word for 'what.' Pronunciation changes to 'nan' before certain sounds (like です → なんですか).

examples.jp: なにをたべますか
これはなんですか

nani.notes: なに before を (wo), が (ga). なん before です (desu), の (no), and counters.

doko.description: Question word for 'where.' One of the こそあど (ko-so-a-do) words — ここ (here), そこ (there), あそこ (over there), どこ (where).

examples.jp: えきはどこですか
どこにいきますか

doko.notes: どこ can be made polite by adding でしょうか instead of ですか.

itsu.description: Question word for 'when.' Unlike English, いつ can go almost anywhere in the sentence.

examples.jp: いつにほんにいきますか
examples.en: When are you going to Japan?

examples.jp: たんじょうびはいつですか

itsu.notes: いつも (itsumo) means 'always.' いつか (itsuka) means 'someday.'

naze.description: The most standard way to say 'why.' More formal than なんで. どうして is the polite alternative.

examples.jp: なぜおくれましたか
なぜにほんごをべんきょうしますか

naze.notes: なぜ (formal/written) > どうして (polite) > なんで (casual). Use なんで with friends.

ikutsu.description: Means both 'how many' and 'how old.' Context determines meaning. For age, it's a casual way to ask — おいくつ is polite.

examples.jp: りんごはいくつありますか
おいくつですか

ikutsu.notes: Add お before いくつ (おいくつ) to make it polite when asking age.

ikura.description: Means 'how much' — almost always refers to price. Essential for shopping in Japan.

examples.jp: これはいくらですか
ぜんぶでいくらですか
examples.en: How much is it in total?

ikura.notes: This is one of the most useful phrases for travelers in Japan.

hai.description: The standard word for 'yes.' Also used to show you're listening (like 'uh-huh'). In Japan, はい doesn't always mean agreement — it can just mean 'I hear you.'

examples.jp: はい、そうです
はい、わかりました

hai.notes: Japanese people say はい frequently during conversation to show they're listening. It doesn't always mean 'yes, I agree.'

iie.description: The standard word for 'no.' Can also be used as 'not at all' or 'don't mention it' when someone thanks you.

examples.jp: いいえ、ちがいます
いいえ、けっこうです

iie.notes: Japanese culture often avoids direct 'no.' You'll hear ちょっと... (chotto) as a softer way to decline.

watashi.description: The most common first-person pronoun, meaning 'I' or 'me.' Safe to use in any situation. Used by both men and women.

examples.jp: わたしはがくせいです
わたしのなまえはたなかです

watashi.notes: In casual speech, Japanese people often drop the pronoun entirely since it's understood from context.

boku.description: A casual first-person pronoun used mainly by males. Softer and more boyish than おれ. Common among young men and boys.

examples.jp: ぼくはにほんじんです
ぼくのいえはとおい

boku.notes: Used by males. Using ぼく in a formal business setting would be too casual. Some women use it for a tomboyish effect.

atashi.description: A softer, more feminine version of わたし. Used almost exclusively by women and girls in casual situations.

examples.jp: あたしもいく
あたしのことすき？

atashi.notes: Very feminine. Male speakers never use this. Considered cute/girly.

kare.description: Means 'he' but also commonly means 'boyfriend.' Context determines which meaning is intended.

examples.jp: かれはせんせいです
かれとえいがをみた
examples.en: I watched a movie with my boyfriend.

kare.notes: かれし (kareshi) specifically means 'boyfriend' without ambiguity.

kanojo.description: Means 'she' but also commonly means 'girlfriend.' Like かれ, context determines the meaning.

examples.jp: かのじょはやさしいひとです
かのじょがいますか

kanojo.notes: Japanese often avoids pronouns entirely, referring to people by name + さん instead.

sama.description: The most respectful honorific suffix. Used for customers (お客様), God (神様), and people of very high status. Much more formal than さん.

examples.jp: おきゃくさま
たなかさま、おまちしておりました
examples.en: Mr. Tanaka, we've been expecting you.

sama.notes: You'll hear this constantly in shops and restaurants. Never use it for yourself.

san.description: The most common honorific suffix, similar to Mr./Mrs./Ms. Used with last names, first names, and even company names. Safe default for anyone.

examples.jp: たなかさん
やまださんはどこですか

san.notes: Never use さん for yourself. It's like calling yourself 'Mr. Me.' Use it for everyone else as default.

kun.description: An honorific suffix used mainly for boys and young men, or by superiors addressing male subordinates. Less formal than さん.

examples.jp: たなかくん、きてください
けんくんはどこ？

kun.notes: Teachers use くん for male students. Bosses may use it for younger male employees. Women sometimes use it for close male friends.

chan.description: An affectionate suffix used for children, close friends, babies, and cute things. Very informal and endearing.

examples.jp: ゆきちゃん、あそぼう
あかちゃん

chan.notes: Using ちゃん with someone you don't know well can be rude. It implies closeness or that someone is cute/small.

sensei.description: Means 'teacher' but also used as an honorific for doctors, lawyers, politicians, authors, and martial arts instructors. Shows deep respect for expertise.

examples.jp: せんせい、しつもんがあります
やまだせんせい

sensei.notes: せんせい literally means 'born before' (先生). It's used as both a title and a standalone way to address someone.

senpai.description: Someone senior to you in school, work, or a club. A fundamental concept in Japanese social hierarchy with no English equivalent.

examples.jp: せんぱい、おしえてください
たなかせんぱいはすごい

senpai.notes: The senpai-kouhai relationship is central to Japanese culture. Senpai are expected to guide kouhai, and kouhai show respect.

kouhai.description: Someone junior to you — the opposite of せんぱい. Your kouhai in school or work are people who started after you.

examples.jp: こうはいのめんどうをみる
かれはわたしのこうはいです

kouhai.notes: Unlike senpai, kouhai is rarely used as a form of address. You wouldn't call someone 'Kouhai.'

otona.description: Means 'adult.' Also used figuratively to describe mature behavior — おとなっぽい means 'adult-like' or 'mature.'

examples.jp: おとなになりたい
examples.en: I want to become an adult.

examples.jp: おとなはにせんえんです

otona.notes: In Japan, you legally become an adult at 18 (changed from 20 in 2022).

kodomo.description: Means 'child' or 'children.' こどもの日 (Kodomo no Hi) is Children's Day, a national holiday on May 5th.

examples.jp: こどもがさんにんいます
こどものとき

kodomo.notes: お子さん (okosan) is the polite way to refer to someone else's children.

inu.description: Dog. Japan has a deep cultural connection with dogs — the story of Hachiko, the loyal Akita, is famous worldwide.

examples.jp: いぬがすきです
おおきいいぬがいる

inu.notes: Japanese dog breeds include Shiba Inu, Akita, and Tosa. わんわん (wan wan) is the Japanese onomatopoeia for barking.

neko.description: Cat. Cats are deeply embedded in Japanese culture — from 招き猫 (maneki neko, beckoning cat) to cat cafes.

examples.jp: ねこをかっています
あのねこはかわいい

neko.notes: にゃー (nyaa) is the Japanese onomatopoeia for meowing. Japan has thousands of cat cafes.

tori.description: Bird. Also means chicken when referring to food (とりにく = chicken meat).

examples.jp: とりがないている
とりにくをたべます

tori.notes: とり for food vs. にわとり specifically for a live chicken. The kanji 鳥 is used for birds in general.

sakana.description: Fish. Japan is surrounded by ocean, and fish is central to Japanese cuisine — sushi, sashimi, grilled fish.

examples.jp: さかなをたべるのがすき
うみにさかながいる
examples.en: There are fish in the ocean.

sakana.notes: The kanji 魚 appears in many food-related words. おさかな (osakana) is the polite form.

mizu.description: Water. In Japanese restaurants, water is free and automatically served. おみず (omizu) is the polite form.

examples.jp: みずをください
みずがつめたい

mizu.notes: おゆ (oyu) means hot water. In restaurants, you can say おみずください (omizu kudasai) for free water.

gohan.description: Means both 'cooked rice' and 'meal' in general. Rice is so central to Japanese food that the word for rice IS the word for meal.

examples.jp: ごはんをたべましたか
examples.en: Have you eaten? (lit. Have you eaten rice?)

examples.jp: あさごはん

gohan.notes: あさごはん = breakfast, ひるごはん = lunch, ばんごはん = dinner. こめ (kome) refers to uncooked rice.

tabemono.description: Food. Formed from たべる (to eat) + もの (thing) — literally 'eating thing.'

examples.jp: にほんのたべものがすきです
すきなたべものはなんですか

tabemono.notes: Many Japanese nouns are formed this way: のみもの (drink = drinking thing), かいもの (shopping = buying thing).

nomimono.description: Drink/beverage. From のむ (to drink) + もの (thing).

examples.jp: のみものはなにがいいですか
examples.en: What would you like to drink?

examples.jp: つめたいのみものをください

nomimono.notes: In Japanese vending machines (じどうはんばいき), you'll find a huge variety of のみもの.

isha.description: Doctor. One of the most common occupations you'll need to know. おいしゃさん (oisha-san) is the polite form most people use.

examples.jp: いしゃにいきます
おいしゃさんはなんといいましたか

isha.notes: People usually say おいしゃさん (with お and さん) in daily conversation. Just いしゃ can sound blunt.

kazoku.description: Family. Japanese has different words for family members depending on whether you're talking about your own family (humble) or someone else's (honorific).

examples.jp: かぞくはよにんです
examples.en: There are four people in my family.

examples.jp: かぞくにあいたい
examples.en: I want to see my family.

kazoku.notes: ごかぞく (go-kazoku) is the polite form when asking about someone else's family.

chichi.description: Father — used when talking ABOUT your own father to others. To address your father directly, use おとうさん (otousan).

examples.jp: ちちはかいしゃいんです
examples.en: My father is a company employee.

examples.jp: ちちにきいてみます

chichi.notes: ちち = my father (humble). おとうさん = someone else's father (polite) or when calling your own dad.

haha.description: Mother — used when talking ABOUT your own mother to others. To address her directly, use おかあさん (okaasan).

examples.jp: はははりょうりがじょうずです
examples.en: My mother is good at cooking.

examples.jp: ははのひ

haha.notes: はは = my mother (humble). おかあさん = someone else's mother (polite) or when calling your own mom.

ani.description: Older brother — humble form for YOUR older brother. Use おにいさん (oniisan) for someone else's older brother or to address yours.

examples.jp: あにはとうきょうにすんでいます
examples.en: My older brother lives in Tokyo.

examples.jp: あにはさんさいうえです
examples.en: My brother is three years older.

ani.notes: Japanese distinguishes older/younger siblings: あに (older bro), おとうと (younger bro), あね (older sis), いもうと (younger sis).

ane.description: Older sister — humble form. Use おねえさん (oneesan) for someone else's older sister or to address yours directly.

examples.jp: あねはけっこんしています
あねにもらいました
examples.en: I received it from my sister.

ane.notes: おねえさん can also be used to address a young woman you don't know, similar to 'miss.'

taberu.description: To eat. An ichidan (ru-verb) — one of the easiest verb types to conjugate. Just drop る and add endings.

examples.jp: すしをたべます
なにをたべたいですか
examples.en: What do you want to eat?

taberu.notes: Ichidan verb. たべます (polite), たべた (past), たべない (negative), たべて (te-form). めしあがる (meshiagaru) is the honorific form.

nomu.description: To drink. A godan (u-verb). Also means 'to swallow' or 'to take medicine.'

examples.jp: おちゃをのみます
くすりをのんでください

nomu.notes: Godan verb. のみます (polite), のんだ (past), のまない (negative). Note the ん in past tense.

iku.description: To go. One of the most essential verbs. Irregular in te-form: いって (itte) not いいて.

examples.jp: がっこうにいきます
いっしょにいきましょう

iku.notes: Godan verb with irregular te-form. いきます (polite), いった (past), いかない (negative), いって (te-form).

kuru.description: To come. One of only two irregular verbs in Japanese (the other is する). Essential to memorize all its forms.

examples.jp: ともだちがきます
にほんにきてよかった
examples.en: I'm glad I came to Japan.

kuru.notes: Irregular verb. きます (polite), きた (past), こない (negative), きて (te-form). Completely irregular — must memorize.

suru.description: To do. The other irregular verb. Extremely versatile — attaches to nouns to create verbs: べんきょうする (to study), りょこうする (to travel).

examples.jp: なにをしますか
べんきょうをする

suru.notes: Irregular verb. します (polite), した (past), しない (negative), して (te-form). Creates hundreds of compound verbs.

miru.description: To see / to look / to watch. Ichidan verb. Used for movies, TV, scenery — anything visual.

examples.jp: テレビをみます
ふじさんをみたい
examples.en: I want to see Mt. Fuji.

miru.notes: Ichidan verb. みます (polite), みた (past), みない (negative). ごらんになる (goran ni naru) is the honorific form.

kiku.description: Means both 'to listen' and 'to ask.' Context determines meaning. One verb, two important uses.

examples.jp: おんがくをきく
せんせいにきいてください

kiku.notes: Godan verb. For 'to ask,' you use に to mark who you're asking. For 'to listen,' you use を for what you're listening to.

hanasu.description: To speak / to talk. Godan verb. はなし (hanashi) as a noun means 'story' or 'conversation.'

examples.jp: にほんごをはなします
ちょっとはなしがあります
examples.en: I have something to talk about.

hanasu.notes: Godan verb. はなします (polite), はなした (past), はなさない (negative). おはなし is polite for 'story.'

kaku.description: To write. Godan verb. Also used for drawing (えをかく = to draw a picture).

examples.jp: てがみをかきます
なまえをかいてください

kaku.notes: Godan verb. かきます (polite), かいた (past), かかない (negative), かいて (te-form).

yomu.description: To read. Godan verb. Essential for talking about books, manga, newspapers, and signs.

examples.jp: ほんをよみます
まんがをよむのがすき

yomu.notes: Godan verb. よみます (polite), よんだ (past), よまない (negative). Note: past tense is よんだ (n-sound).

aruku.description: To walk. Godan verb. さんぽする (sanpo suru) means 'to take a walk' and is more leisurely.

examples.jp: がっこうまであるきます
いっしょにあるきましょう

aruku.notes: Godan verb. あるきます (polite), あるいた (past), あるかない (negative).

neru.description: To sleep / to go to bed. Ichidan verb. Can mean both the act of sleeping and going to bed.

examples.jp: もうねます
ゆうべよくねた

neru.notes: Ichidan verb. ねます (polite), ねた (past), ねない (negative). おやすみなさい is what you say before bed.

ookii.description: Big / large. An i-adjective. Can describe size, volume, or scale. おおきな (ookina) is the pre-noun form.

examples.jp: おおきいいえにすんでいます
examples.en: I live in a big house.

examples.jp: こえがおおきい

ookii.notes: I-adjective. おおきくない (negative), おおきかった (past). おおきな can go directly before nouns.

chiisai.description: Small / little. I-adjective. The opposite of おおきい. ちいさな (chiisana) is the pre-noun form.

examples.jp: ちいさいねこがいる
こえがちいさい

chiisai.notes: I-adjective. ちいさくない (negative), ちいさかった (past).

ii.description: Good / fine / nice. Irregular i-adjective — its negative is よくない (yokunai), not いくない. One of the most used words.

examples.jp: いいてんきですね
それはいいですね

ii.notes: IRREGULAR: いい → よくない (neg), よかった (past). The original form is よい (yoi). いいです can also mean 'no thank you.'

warui.description: Bad. I-adjective. Also used as an informal apology — わるい！(warui!) means 'my bad!'

examples.jp: てんきがわるい
わるいけど、いけない

warui.notes: I-adjective. わるくない (negative), わるかった (past). わるい！as slang = 'sorry!' or 'my bad!'

atarashii.description: New. I-adjective. The opposite is ふるい (furui = old, for things). Don't confuse with あたたかい (warm).

examples.jp: あたらしいくるまをかいました
あたらしいともだちができた

atarashii.notes: I-adjective. Only for things, not people. For people's age, use わかい (young) vs としをとった (old).

atsui.description: Hot. Can mean hot weather (暑い) or hot to touch (熱い). Same pronunciation, different kanji and usage.

examples.jp: きょうはあついですね
コーヒーがあつい

atsui.notes: 暑い = hot weather/climate. 熱い = hot to touch (food, drinks). They sound identical but mean different things.

samui.description: Cold (weather/temperature). For cold objects or drinks, use つめたい (tsumetai) instead.

examples.jp: きょうはさむいですね
ふゆはさむい

samui.notes: さむい = cold weather. つめたい = cold to touch (drinks, ice). Don't mix them up!

hayai.description: Can mean 'fast' (速い) or 'early' (早い). Same pronunciation, different kanji. Context determines meaning.

examples.jp: あさはやくおきます
examples.en: I wake up early in the morning.

examples.jp: このでんしゃははやい

hayai.notes: 早い = early (time). 速い = fast (speed). はやくして！ means 'hurry up!'

takai.description: Means both 'tall/high' and 'expensive.' One of the most useful i-adjectives.

examples.jp: このやまはたかい
にほんはものがたかい

takai.notes: たかい = tall/high AND expensive. せがたかい = tall (person). ねだんがたかい = expensive.

yasui.description: Cheap / inexpensive. The opposite of たかい (expensive). As a verb suffix, ～やすい means 'easy to do.'

examples.jp: このレストランはやすい
つかいやすい

yasui.notes: As standalone = cheap. As suffix ～やすい = easy to (たべやすい = easy to eat, わかりやすい = easy to understand).

ame.description: Rain. Japan has a rainy season (梅雨 tsuyu) in June-July. Rain is a common topic of conversation.

examples.jp: あめがふっています
あしたはあめです

ame.notes: あめ also means 'candy' with different pitch accent. つゆ (tsuyu) is the rainy season. かさ (kasa) = umbrella.

yuki.description: Snow. Northern Japan (Hokkaido) gets heavy snowfall. Snow festivals are major cultural events.

examples.jp: ゆきがふっています
ゆきまつりにいきたい
examples.en: I want to go to the snow festival.

yuki.notes: さっぽろゆきまつり (Sapporo Snow Festival) is one of Japan's biggest winter events.

hana.description: Flower. Also means 'nose' (鼻) with different kanji. Flower viewing (花見 hanami) is a beloved Japanese tradition.

examples.jp: はながきれいです
はなみにいきましょう

hana.notes: 花 (flower) vs 鼻 (nose) — same pronunciation. はなみ (hanami) = cherry blossom viewing, a spring tradition.

yama.description: Mountain. Japan is 73% mountainous. ふじさん (Fuji-san) is the most famous mountain and a cultural icon.

examples.jp: やまにのぼります
ふじさんはにほんでいちばんたかいやまです
examples.en: Mt. Fuji is the tallest mountain in Japan.

yama.notes: ふじさん uses さん meaning 'mountain,' not the honorific さん. やまのぼり = mountain climbing.

umi.description: Ocean / sea. Japan is an island nation surrounded by sea, so うみ is culturally significant.

examples.jp: うみでおよぎます
うみがみたい
examples.en: I want to see the ocean.

umi.notes: うみべ (umibe) = seaside/beach. Japanese beaches are popular in summer (なつ).

aka.description: Red. In Japanese culture, red symbolizes vitality, happiness, and good luck. Torii gates, daruma dolls, and envelopes for celebrations are red.

examples.jp: あかいくるま
あかちゃん

aka.notes: あかい is the i-adjective form. Red is associated with celebrations and shrine gates (とりい).

ao.description: Blue. Interestingly, あお in Japanese traditionally covers both blue AND green. Traffic lights in Japan are called あお (blue), not みどり (green).

examples.jp: そらがあおい
しんごうがあおになった
examples.en: The traffic light turned green (lit. blue).

ao.notes: Japanese traditionally didn't distinguish blue from green. あおしんごう = 'green' light. あおりんご = green apple.

shiro.description: White. Symbolizes purity and cleanliness in Japanese culture. Brides wear white at Shinto weddings.

examples.jp: しろいねこ
しろいごはん

shiro.notes: しろい is the i-adjective form. おしろ (oshiro) with different kanji means 'castle.'

kuro.description: Black. In Japanese culture, black can symbolize formality (suits) or mystery. くろおび (kuro-obi) is a black belt in martial arts.

examples.jp: くろいかみ
くろおびをもっています

kuro.notes: くろい is the i-adjective form. Most Japanese people have くろいかみ (black hair).

jikan.description: Time / hour. Used both for the concept of time and as a counter for hours.

examples.jp: じかんがない
にじかんかかります

jikan.notes: いちじかん = one hour, にじかん = two hours. じかんがある = to have time, じかんがない = to not have time.

asa.description: Morning. Japanese mornings often start early — many businesses open at 8 or 9 AM.

examples.jp: あさはやくおきます
examples.en: I wake up early in the morning.

examples.jp: あさごはんをたべましたか

asa.notes: あさごはん = breakfast. まいあさ = every morning. あさって = the day after tomorrow (different word!).

dou.description: Question word for 'how' — asks about manner, condition, or opinion. どうですか (dou desu ka) = 'How is it?' / 'What do you think?'

examples.jp: どうですか
どうしましたか

ikaga.description: The polite form of どう (dou). Used in formal situations, restaurants, and customer service. いかがですか = 'How would you like it?'

examples.jp: おちゃはいかがですか
ごきぶんはいかがですか

docchi.description: Casual way to ask 'which one?' between two options. The polite form is どちら (dochira).

examples.jp: どっちがすき？
みぎとひだり、どっち？

dore.description: Question word for 'which one?' when there are three or more options. Part of the ko-so-a-do system: これ (this), それ (that), あれ (that over there), どれ (which).

examples.jp: どれがいいですか
すきなのはどれですか
examples.rom: suki na no wa dore desu ka

un.description: A Japanese word meaning "yeah." Practice using it in sentences.

examples.jp: うんはにほんごで「yeah」です
うんをおぼえました

ee.description: A Japanese word meaning "yeah." Practice using it in sentences.

examples.jp: ええはにほんごで「yeah」です
ええをおぼえました

uun.description: A Japanese word meaning "nah." Practice using it in sentences.

examples.jp: ううんはにほんごで「nah」です
ううんをおぼえました

Ohayou gozaimasu.description: A polite morning greeting used until about 10-11 AM. The casual version is just おはよう (ohayou).

examples.jp: あさ、ともだちに「おはようございます」といいます
examples.en: In the morning, you say "Good morning" to friends.

Konban wa.description: An evening greeting, used after sunset. Unlike English, this is only used when meeting someone, not when parting.

examples.jp: よる、ひとにあったら「こんばんは」といいます
examples.en: When you meet someone at night, you say "Good evening."

Oyasuminasai.description: Said before going to bed, or when parting at night. The casual form is just おやすみ (oyasumi).

examples.jp: ねるまえに「おやすみなさい」といいます
examples.en: You say "Good night" before going to sleep.

Mata ashita.description: See you tomorrow. Used at the end of the day with people you'll see the next day — classmates, coworkers.

もっとべんきょうしなきゃ

sumimasen.description: One of the most useful Japanese words. Means 'excuse me,' 'sorry,' and even 'thank you' (for the trouble). Used constantly in daily life.

examples.jp: すみません、えきはどこですか
examples.en: Excuse me, where is the station?
examples.rom: sumimasen, eki wa doko desu ka

examples.jp: すみません、おくれました

ganbatte.description: Do your best! / Good luck! / Hang in there! A very common encouragement phrase. Japanese say this before exams, sports, work.

examples.jp: しけんがんばってね
がんばります！

onegaishimasu.description: Please (when requesting something). Literally 'I humbly request.' One of the most important polite phrases in Japanese.

examples.jp: みずをおねがいします
よろしくおねがいします
examples.en: Nice to meet you. / Please treat me well.

itadakimasu.description: Said before every meal. Literally 'I humbly receive.' Expresses gratitude for the food, the cook, and the ingredients. Essential Japanese manners.

examples.jp: いただきます！

gochisousama.description: Said after finishing a meal. Thanks the cook and acknowledges the effort of the meal. The polite form adds でした (deshita).

examples.jp: ごちそうさまでした
examples.en: Thank you for the meal. (after eating)
